abstract.t: This document is the PIC Sandboxed Execution Specification, a subordinate specification of the PIC Specification. It retains its historical repository filename (pic-lineage-guardrail-spec.md).

A Multi-Lineage Execution carries one or more independent PIC lineages together for one proposed transition. When such a transition must be validated and controlled by configured policy, that control is itself an execution — and PIC governs it the way it governs any other: as a lineage.

A Sandboxed Execution places the Multi-Lineage Execution inside an ordinary outer PIC lineage. Each guardrail is a normal executor of that outer lineage: it verifies the outer continuation, verifies every carried inner lineage, applies the enforcement function, and — on permit — proves the next ordinary outer PCA.

No new PCA type is introduced, no trusted sandbox, and no external guardrail authority. The outer lineage is identified only by the operation ENFORCE and by one signed profile field, multiLineage, that carries the inner execution. The construction is recursive: an inner leg may itself be a Sandboxed Execution.

li.t: A Sandboxed Execution is PIC applied recursively: an outer PIC execution carries and governs an inner PIC execution.

abstract.t: Guardrails build on the PCA format of the PIC Prover and Verifier Specification and are consistent with the PIC Revocation Specification; they do not alter Proof of Relationship, non-expansion, or the rule that every PCA continues exactly one predecessor. In case of conflict, the PIC Specification is authoritative.

note.title: Document Status
note.t: Version: 0.2 (Draft) Status: Draft – Not a Standard Date: 2026-07-20 Source: github.com/pic-protocol/pic-spec/draft/0.2/pic-lineage-guardrail-spec.md

This revision defines the Sandboxed Execution: PIC applied recursively — an outer PIC lineage that carries and governs an inner Multi-Lineage Execution. It introduces no new PCA type and no trusted sandbox.

list.t: Nicola Gallo (Nitro Agility S.r.l.) Lead Editor

t.em: Add your name via pull request (individual or organization) — listing is subject to editor approval (see ).
Add your name via pull request (individual or organization) — listing is subject to editor approval (see ).

section.name: Introduction
section.t: This section is non-normative. It introduces the three concepts this specification builds on, and the single idea that connects them: PIC carries PIC.

figure.sourcecode:
LINEAGE EXECUTION ----------------- one origin; one predecessor per PCA; non-expansion
MULTI-LINEAGE EXECUTION ----------------------- n >= 1 independent Lineage Executions carried together
SANDBOXED EXECUTION ------------------- an outer PIC Lineage Execution carrying a Multi-Lineage Execution

section.t: The whole construction reduces to one picture:

figure.sourcecode: Sandboxed Execution = outer PIC lineage carrying inner Multi-Lineage Execution

section.t: The outer lineage applies PIC recursively to the execution that validates and controls the inner execution.

section.name: Lineage Execution

li.t: A Lineage Execution is a single-origin PIC execution in which every PCA continues exactly one predecessor under PoR, and authority remains bounded by the origin authority context.

list.t: one origin, PCA0;
every later PCA continues exactly one predecessor, witnessed by PoR;
non-expansion at every hop, so authority only narrows;
no authority is imported from another lineage;
physical executor behavior is outside the protocol guarantee.

figure.sourcecode:
PCA0-A { READ, BACKUP } | | PoR + non-expansion v PCA1-A { BACKUP } | v NEXT EXECUTOR
One origin. One predecessor per PCA. Authority only narrows.

section.t: The construction — PCA format, Prover, Verifier — is defined by the PIC Prover and Verifier Specification; revocation by the PIC Revocation Specification; the formal model and proofs by the PIC Model [1]. This document restates none of them.

li.t: A Multi-Lineage Execution carries one or more independent Lineage Executions together for one proposed transition, without merging their authorities.

list.t: it carries n >= 1 independent PIC lineages;
each lineage remains independently verifiable;
it has no combined authority of its own;
it is the execution input carried by the multiLineage field (Section 2.3).

figure.sourcecode:
+======================================+ | MULTI-LINEAGE EXECUTION | | | | leg A: PCA1-A { BACKUP } | | leg B: PCA0-B { WRITE-S3 } | | | | authorities remain separate | +======================================+
Distinct lineages, one proposed transition.

section.name: Sandboxed Guardrails
section.t: A Sandboxed Execution is a multi-hop outer PIC execution. Its executors are guardrails, and each continues the previous one under PoR:

figure.sourcecode:
OUTER SANDBOXED EXECUTION
PCA-G[n-1] PCA-G[n] PCA-G[n+1] Guardrail n-1 --PoR--> Guardrail n --PoR--> Guardrail n+1 | | carries and evaluates v Multi-Lineage Execution [ leg A | leg B | ... ]

section.t: Each guardrail:

list.t: receives an ordinary outer PCA carrying multiLineage;
verifies the outer PIC continuation;
verifies every inner leg;
applies the enforcement function;
on permit, proves the next ordinary outer PCA;
on deny, produces no authorizing continuation for that crossing.

li.t: A guardrail is an executor of a Sandboxed Execution: it verifies the outer continuation, verifies the carried inner execution, applies the enforcement function, and, on permit, proves the next ordinary outer PCA.

section.t: An execution is sandboxed not because a physical boundary forced guardrail invocation, but because the next conforming guardrail accepts and continues only a valid outer PCA.

li.t: The executor is not physically sandboxed by this specification. The execution is sandboxed because it can continue only through valid guardrail hops of the outer PIC lineage.

section.name: Requirements Notation
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [2] [3] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals. Examples are illustrative and non-normative.

section.name: Outer and Inner Execution

figure.sourcecode: outer execution = Sandboxed Execution inner execution = Multi-Lineage Execution carried in multiLineage

section.t: The outer execution is an ordinary Lineage Execution with its own PCA0, authority context, PoR chain, execution contract, continuation, and revocation coordinates. Every inner leg independently has the same PIC structure.
The outer execution does not absorb the inner lineages. It carries them as execution inputs and continues on its own authority, ENFORCE.

figure.sourcecode: Outer authority = ENFORCE Inner lineages = execution inputs carried for evaluation Outer authority != union of inner authorities

section.name: PIC Carrying PIC
section.t: The execution being controlled is already represented through PIC. The guardrail process that controls it must itself be controlled, across guardrails not yet known. So the controlled Multi-Lineage Execution becomes the signed subject of another PIC lineage — and that lineage can be carried again.

figure.sourcecode: OUTER PIC PCA-G[n] | +-- operation: ENFORCE +-- executionContract: required next-guardrail properties +-- multiLineage: | +-- INNER PIC LEG A: PCA-A[x] +-- INNER PIC LEG B: PCA-B[y] +-- ...
PCA-G[n-1] --> PCA-G[n] --> PCA-G[n+1] | +-- multiLineage | +-- independently verified PIC legs

li.t: PIC does not stop at protecting application execution. It can protect the execution that protects application execution.

section.name: The multiLineage PCA Profile Field
section.t: multiLineage is a signed profile extension of an ordinary PCA, in the same extension style as the revocation coordinates of the PIC Revocation Specification, Section 2. It carries one Multi-Lineage Execution; inside it, legs is the bounded list of independently verifiable PIC inputs.
Semantics:

list.t: the whole multiLineage field is covered by the outer PCA's ordinary single signature;
every leg is an ordinary PIC PCA, or the profile-selected proof representation of one;
each leg keeps its own origin, predecessor relation, authority context, and revocation coordinates;
legs are never merged;
the list is bounded by the profile;
a leg MAY itself contain multiLineage; recursion has no special terminal depth in the model;
implementations MAY impose resource and maximum-depth limits; these are implementation and profile limits, not PIC-semantic changes.

section.t: multiLineage MUST NOT alter previousPcaHash, create additional predecessors, enter invariants.operations, import authority from its legs, or replace PoR, any leg's signature, or any leg's validation.

Exactness. The outer request binding commits to the complete presented multiLineage. The Verifier reconstructs the leg set and context from the presented objects and recomputes every supplied digest; an added, removed, or substituted leg causes rejection.

li.t: Exactness applies to the authenticated Multi-Lineage Execution presented to the guardrail. Detecting inputs hidden before presentation requires a profile-defined authenticated input manifest or observation source.

section.t: The recursion is structural, not authority composition:

figure.sourcecode: PCA +-- multiLineage +-- leg: PCA +-- leg: PCA +-- leg: PCA +-- multiLineage +-- ...

section.name: Originating the Sandboxed Execution
section.t: An executor that wants its proposed Multi-Lineage Execution to enter the first guardrail creates an ordinary origin PCA, PCA0-G — provided it is authorized to originate that outer lineage. A component not authorized to mint the outer PCA0 cannot self-authorize by creating one.

PCA0-G is an ordinary PCA0: no predecessor, no PoR, signed by its authorized origin. It delegates the future guardrail through the execution contract, without naming it. The first guardrail proves conformance and produces PCA1-G; subsequent guardrails produce PCA2-G, PCA3-G, and so on.

figure.sourcecode: authorized caller creates PCA0-G | | future delegation (execution contract) v unknown guardrail proves conformance and creates PCA1-G

section.name: Guardrail Prover and Verifier Profile
section.t: A guardrail performs two nested verification levels over the existing ordinary PIC procedures, then — on permit — proves the next PCA.
Outer verification. The guardrail acts as an ordinary PIC Verifier for the outer execution:

list.t: validate the outer predecessor PCA;
verify outer PoR and predecessor binding;
verify continuation freshness;
verify the guardrail executor attestation and conformance to the inherited execution contract;
verify non-expansion;
verify temporal and revocation requirements;
verify that the outer request matches the presented multiLineage.

section.t: Inner verification. Only after outer validation succeeds, the guardrail validates every leg in multiLineage with the ordinary PIC Verifier procedure and the selected chain representation: origin or immediate predecessor, signature, PoR, predecessor binding, non-expansion, execution-contract conformance, request binding, freshness, and revocation. Any invalid leg is deny, before policy.

Enforcement function. Over the validated legs, their bound scopes and metadata, and the exact crossing context — destination, requests, payload commitments — the guardrail evaluates the profile-defined enforcement function. A PDP or policy engine is one possible implementation.

li.t: The profile requires an enforcement result; it does not require a particular PDP architecture, policy language, product, or deployment topology.

section.t: Failure, unavailable policy, indeterminate evaluation, or inconsistent input mapping default to deny.

Outer proving. On permit, the guardrail acts as ordinary PIC Prover: it constructs the next outer PCA, responds to the predecessor challenge, keeps or attenuates { ENFORCE }, preserves or strengthens the execution contract, carries the exact output multiLineage, binds the enforcement result and exact context in the ordinary request, adds a fresh continuation, and signs once. The next guardrail repeats the same procedure — and that repetition is the sandbox.

figure.sourcecode: receive outer PCA | validate outer PIC continuation | validate every inner PIC leg | apply enforcement function | permit? / \ no yes X build and sign next ordinary outer PCA

section.name: Operation Profile
section.t: This revision uses one operation class, ENFORCE:

list.t: it means: validate the carried Multi-Lineage Execution, apply the enforcement function, and decide whether the outer execution may continue;
it grants none of the authority carried by the inner legs;
it is subject to ordinary PIC non-expansion: the outer lineage MAY retain or attenuate it, never add broader operations.

section.t: Future revisions MAY define additional recursive-control operation classes, using the same ordinary PCA, PoR, and profile-extension model.

section.name: Acceptance
section.t: A conforming guardrail or receiving hop operating under this profile MUST accept the outer continuation only if:

figure.sourcecode: ValidOuterPIC(PCA-G[n-1], PCA-G[n]) AND PCA-G[n].operation == ENFORCE AND ValidMultiLineage(PCA-G[n].multiLineage) AND ExactPresentedExecutionBinding AND EnforcementResult == permit AND Fresh AND NotRevoked

section.t: ValidMultiLineage means every presented leg validates independently under its own PIC lineage and selected validation profile.
The receiving component MUST recompute all digests and MUST reject:

list.t: a missing multiLineage;
an invalid outer continuation;
an invalid inner leg;
an added, removed, or substituted presented leg;
a mismatched request, destination, or payload;
an invalid policy or input commitment;
a non-permit decision;
stale, replayed, or revoked state.

section.t: This profile adds no second signature system: guardrail approval is the ordinary outer PCA signature, nothing else.

section.name: Bypass
section.t: A sender MAY physically skip a guardrail and call the next one with the inner execution alone. The model does not prevent the attempt at the faulty sender; the next conforming guardrail rejects it, because a valid outer continuation is missing.

figure.sourcecode:
BYPASS ATTEMPT
executor ---- inner Multi-Lineage only ----> next guardrail reject: no valid outer continuation

li.t: The failure is not prevented at the faulty hop; it is blocked at the next conforming one.

section.t: For a non-PIC-aware physical target, restricting direct paths to it is a deployment responsibility, addressed by the PIC Architecture and Deployment Specification. This specification does not depend on gateways, service meshes, or physical sandboxes.

section.name: Recursion
section.t: The model is recursively closed. The same PCA format, PoR semantics, non-expansion rule, and Verifier logic apply at every level. No special root guardrail object exists, and no new trust primitive appears at deeper levels.

figure.sourcecode: PCA outer +-- multiLineage +-- PCA inner A +-- PCA inner B +-- multiLineage +-- deeper PIC execution

section.t: The model is recursively composable to arbitrary finite depth — not infinite computation. Implementations MUST bound leg counts, encoded size, and validation depth to prevent resource exhaustion.

section.name: Model Guarantee
section.t: Within the accepted model:

list.t: an outer hop without valid PoR cannot continue;
an unauthorized guardrail cannot become a valid successor;
the outer authority cannot expand beyond ENFORCE;
an invalid inner PCA causes rejection;
a different inner execution cannot be substituted without invalidating the signed binding;
skipping guardrails cannot produce a valid outer continuation.

section.name: Implementation Failure
section.t: The guarantee can fail physically if cryptographic primitives fail, keys or attestation issuers are compromised, a Verifier omits required checks, an executor performs a different physical action from the signed one, or canonicalization or digest construction is implemented incorrectly. These break the implementation or its assumptions, not the model.

section.name: Semantic Divergence
section.t: The enforcement function may be incorrect, dishonest, or semantically divergent. Signing the policy identifier, version, input commitments, semantic profile, and result makes the decision attributable and checkable. PIC proves the continuity and integrity of that decision; it does not prove that a human policy or its interpretation is correct.

section.name: Contributors
section.t: The editors and contributors of this document are listed in the document header above. Listing is governed by Appendix B.7 of the PIC Legal Appendices.

section.name: Legal Notices
section.t: The appendices governing:

list.t: A. Use of Automated Language Assistance,
B. Authorship, Stewardship, Attribution, and Derivative Works,
C. Disclaimer and Limitation of Liability,
D. Acknowledgements,

section.t: are maintained in a single canonical document, the PIC Legal Appendices (draft/0.2/pic-legal.md), and are incorporated into this specification by reference as if fully set forth herein.
In case of conflict between this document and the PIC Legal Appendices, the PIC Legal Appendices prevail for legal, governance, licensing, and attribution matters.

This specification is subordinate to the PIC Specification, which defines the normative semantics of the PIC Model and is the entry point of the specification set. This document does not introduce new conceptual authority, invariants, or authorship claims beyond those defined in the PIC Legal Appendices.

list.t: [1] Gallo, N. (2026). Proof-of-Continuity: A Temporal Model for Authority Propagation in Distributed Systems and AI Agents. arXiv:2607.08906 [cs.CR]. arxiv.org/abs/2607.08906
[2] Bradner, S. (1997). Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels. BCP 14, RFC 2119. rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2119
[3] Leiba, B. (2017). Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words. BCP 14, RFC 8174. rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8174
